terms.definition: A single vowel phonogram with 3 sounds: /a/ as in CAT, /ay/ as in CAKE (long A), and /ah/ as in DAD. The third most used letter in English.
terms.exampleUsage: CAT has short /a/, CAKE has long /ay/ because of silent E, and FATHER has the broad /ah/ sound.

terms.definition: A single vowel phonogram with 2 sounds: /e/ as in BED and /ee/ as in BE. The most common letter in the entire English language. Also serves as silent E with 5 different jobs.
terms.exampleUsage: BED has short /e/ in a closed syllable, while BE has long /ee/ at the end of a word.

terms.definition: A single vowel phonogram with 2 sounds: /i/ as in SIT and /eye/ as in KITE. Short I is one of the trickiest sounds for non-native speakers.
terms.exampleUsage: SIT has short /i/ because the syllable is closed, while KITE has long /eye/ because of silent E.

terms.definition: A single vowel phonogram with 3 sounds: /o/ as in DOG, /oh/ as in GO (long O), and /oo/ as in DO. The oldest letter in the alphabet.
terms.exampleUsage: DOG has short /o/, HOME has long /oh/ from silent E, and TO has the /oo/ sound.

terms.definition: A single vowel phonogram with 2 sounds: /u/ as in CUP and /yoo/ as in USE. U always follows Q in English words.
terms.exampleUsage: CUP has short /u/ and USE has long /yoo/. In BLUE, U gets E as a companion (Rule 3).

terms.definition: A unique phonogram that is both a consonant AND a vowel with 4 sounds: /y/ as in YES (consonant), /ee/ as in HAPPY, /eye/ as in FLY, and /i/ as in GYM.
terms.exampleUsage: Y says /y/ at the start (YES), /ee/ at the end of multi-syllable words (HAPPY), /eye/ at the end of one-syllable words (FLY).

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /b/ as in BAT. Silent in words ending in MB like LAMB and CLIMB.
terms.exampleUsage: BAT, BIG, and BUS all start with the /b/ sound. In THUMB, the B is silent.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 2 sounds: /k/ as in CAT (before A, O, U, or consonants) and /s/ as in ICE (before E, I, Y). Rule 1 governs this with zero exceptions.
terms.exampleUsage: C says /k/ in CAT (before A) and /s/ in CITY (before I). This is Rule 1 — no exceptions!

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /d/ as in DOG. One of the most reliable consonants in English.
terms.exampleUsage: DOG, DIG, and DAD all use the reliable /d/ sound.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /f/ as in FISH. Doubles to FF after a short vowel in one-syllable words (Rule 5).
terms.exampleUsage: FISH and FUN use single F. STAFF and CLIFF use FF after a short vowel (Rule 5).

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 2 sounds: /g/ as in GO and /j/ as in GEM. G tends to soften before E, I, Y but has exceptions (Rule 2).
terms.exampleUsage: GO and BIG have hard /g/. GEM and GIANT have soft /j/. But GET and GIFT keep hard /g/ — exceptions to the softening.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /h/ as in HAT. Silent in HOUR, HONEST, and HEIR. Part of many consonant teams: SH, TH, CH, WH, PH, GH.
terms.exampleUsage: HAT and HELP start with /h/. In HOUR, the H is silent.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /j/ as in JAM. The last letter added to the English alphabet. English words don't end in J (Rule 3).
terms.exampleUsage: JAM, JET, and JOY all have /j/. Words ending in the /j/ sound use DGE (BADGE) or GE (CAGE) instead.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /k/ as in KITE. Silent before N as in KNEE and KNOW (Rule 33).
terms.exampleUsage: KITE and KING start with /k/. In KNEE and KNOW, the K is silent before N.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /l/ as in LIP. Sometimes silent as in WALK and CALM (Rule 34). L warps vowels in ALL, OLL, ULL (Rule 36).
terms.exampleUsage: LIP and LION have clear /l/. WALK has silent L. BALL has L-warped A saying /awl/.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /m/ as in MAP. One of the first sounds babies learn to make.
terms.exampleUsage: MAP, MILK, and MOON all use the /m/ sound. MOM has M at both the start and end.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /n/ as in NET. The 6th most common letter in English.
terms.exampleUsage: NET, NOSE, and NUT all start with /n/. FAN ends with /n/.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /p/ as in PIG. Silent in PSALM, PNEUMONIA, and PSYCHOLOGY.
terms.exampleUsage: PIG, PEN, and POT all have /p/. In PSYCHOLOGY, the P is silent.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram that always appears with U. QU says /kw/ as in QUEEN (Rule 11). No standard English word has Q without U.
terms.exampleUsage: QUEEN, QUICK, and QUIET all have QU saying /kw/. Q never appears alone.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /r/ as in RED. When R follows a vowel, it controls and changes the vowel sound (R-controlled vowels).
terms.exampleUsage: RED and RUN start with /r/. In CAR, the R controls the A to make /ar/.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 2 sounds: /s/ as in SUN and /z/ as in IS. S says /z/ between vowels and after voiced consonants.
terms.exampleUsage: SUN starts with /s/. In IS and DOGS, S says /z/ because it follows voiced sounds.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /t/ as in TOP. Part of many teams: TH, TCH, TI. One of the most common consonants.
terms.exampleUsage: TOP, TEN, and TOY all use /t/. T also appears in teams like TH (THINK) and TCH (MATCH).

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /v/ as in VAN. English words never end in V alone — E is always added (Rule 3).
terms.exampleUsage: VAN and VINE start with /v/. GIVE and HAVE end with VE because V can't end a word alone.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /w/ as in WEB. W warps the A sound toward /o/ in words like WATCH and WANT (Rule 35).
terms.exampleUsage: WEB and WIN start with /w/. In WATCH, W warps A from /a/ toward /o/.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 2 sounds: /ks/ as in BOX (most common) and /z/ as in XYLOPHONE (at start of words).
terms.exampleUsage: BOX and FOX have /ks/ at the end. XYLOPHONE has /z/ at the start — rare position for X.

terms.definition: A single consonant phonogram with 1 sound: /z/ as in ZOO. One of the least used letters in English. Doubles to ZZ after short vowels (Rule 5).
terms.exampleUsage: ZOO and ZERO start with /z/. BUZZ and JAZZ have ZZ after short vowels.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /sh/ as in SHIP. Two letters making one sound that neither S nor H makes alone.
terms.exampleUsage: SHIP starts with SH and FISH ends with SH. The /sh/ sound is completely different from /s/ or /h/.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 2 sounds: voiced /th/ as in THIS (vocal cords vibrate) and unvoiced /th/ as in THINK (breathy, no vibration).
terms.exampleUsage: THIS and THAT have voiced TH (feel your throat vibrate). THINK and MATH have unvoiced TH (breathy).

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 3 sounds: /ch/ as in CHIP (English), /k/ as in SCHOOL (Greek origin), and /sh/ as in CHEF (French origin).
terms.exampleUsage: CHIP uses English /ch/, SCHOOL uses Greek /k/, and CHEF uses French /sh/. Check the word's origin!

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /wh/ as in WHALE. Some dialects pronounce the H breath, others just say /w/.
terms.exampleUsage: WHALE, WHEN, and WHERE all start with the WH team.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /f/ as in PHONE. This is the Greek spelling for the /f/ sound, found in words of Greek origin.
terms.exampleUsage: PHONE, PHOTO, and GRAPH all use PH for /f/. These words come from Greek.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /k/ as in DUCK. Used right after a single short vowel at the end of a one-syllable word (Rule 6). Acts as a bodyguard for the short vowel.
terms.exampleUsage: DUCK, BACK, and KICK all use CK after a short vowel. But BAKE uses just K because the A is long.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /ngk/ as in BANK. The N becomes nasal /ng/ before K.
terms.exampleUsage: BANK, THINK, and INK all have the NK team where N nasalizes to /ng/ before K.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /ng/ as in RING. A nasal sound made at the back of the throat.
terms.exampleUsage: RING, SING, and KING all end with the /ng/ nasal sound.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 2 behaviors: /g/ as in GHOST (at the start of words) and silent as in NIGHT (in the middle/end). Part of larger combos IGH, EIGH, OUGH, AUGH.
terms.exampleUsage: GHOST has GH saying /g/ at the start. In NIGHT, LIGHT, and THOUGHT, GH is silent.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /n/ as in KNEE. The K is always silent before N (Rule 33).
terms.exampleUsage: KNEE, KNOCK, and KNOW all have silent K. The K was once pronounced in Old English!

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /r/ as in WRITE. The W is always silent before R (Rule 33).
terms.exampleUsage: WRITE, WRONG, and WRAP all have silent W before R.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /n/ as in GNAT. The G is silent in GN (Rule 33).
terms.exampleUsage: GNAT, GNAW, and SIGN all have the GN team with silent G.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /j/ as in BADGE. The D shields the short vowel before the /j/ sound (Rule 8). Used only after single short vowels.
terms.exampleUsage: BADGE, BRIDGE, and FUDGE use DGE after short vowels. CAGE and HUGE use just GE after long vowels.

terms.definition: A consonant team phonogram with 1 sound: /ch/ as in MATCH. The T shields the short vowel before the /ch/ sound (Rule 7). Exceptions: SUCH, MUCH, RICH, WHICH.
terms.exampleUsage: MATCH, CATCH, and WATCH use TCH after short vowels. MARCH uses just CH after a consonant.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 1 sound: /ee/ as in TREE. One of the most reliable vowel teams — always says /ee/.
terms.exampleUsage: TREE, BEE, and SEED all have EE reliably saying /ee/.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 3 sounds: /ee/ as in BEAN (most common), /e/ as in BREAD, and /ay/ as in BREAK.
terms.exampleUsage: BEAN and READ (present) have /ee/. BREAD and HEAD have short /e/. BREAK and STEAK have /ay/.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 1 sound: /oh/ as in GOAT. Always says long O — very reliable.
terms.exampleUsage: GOAT, BOAT, and ROAD all have OA reliably saying long /oh/.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 1 sound: /oy/ as in COIN. Used in the MIDDLE of words (Rule 10). Its partner OY is used at the end.
terms.exampleUsage: COIN, JOIN, and BOIL have OI in the middle. For the end, use OY: BOY, TOY.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 1 sound: /oy/ as in BOY. Used at the END of words (Rule 10). Its partner OI is used in the middle.
terms.exampleUsage: BOY, TOY, and JOY have OY at the end. For the middle, use OI: COIN, JOIN.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with multiple sounds: /ow/ as in OUT (most common), /oo/ as in YOU, and /u/ as in TOUCH.
terms.exampleUsage: OUT and HOUSE have /ow/. YOU and YOUTH have /oo/. TOUCH and COUSIN have /u/.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 2 sounds: /ow/ as in COW and /oh/ as in SNOW. Context determines which sound.
terms.exampleUsage: COW and OWL have /ow/. SNOW and GROW have long /oh/. You have to know the word!

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 1 sound: /aw/ as in SAUCE. Used in the MIDDLE of words. Its partner AW is used at the end.
terms.exampleUsage: SAUCE, PAUSE, and HAUNT have AU in the middle.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 1 sound: /aw/ as in SAW. Used at the END of words and before certain consonants. Partner of AU.
terms.exampleUsage: SAW, DRAW, and LAW have AW at or near the end.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 2 sounds: /oo/ as in NEW and /yoo/ as in FEW.
terms.exampleUsage: NEW and BLEW have /oo/. FEW and DEW have /yoo/.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 2 sounds: long /oo/ as in MOON and short /oo/ as in BOOK.
terms.exampleUsage: MOON and FOOD have long /oo/. BOOK and COOK have short /oo/. The two sounds are different!

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 1 sound: /ay/ as in RAIN. Used in the MIDDLE of words (Rule 9). Its partner AY is used at the end.
terms.exampleUsage: RAIN, WAIT, and TRAIN have AI in the middle. For the end, use AY: PLAY, DAY.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 1 sound: /ay/ as in PLAY. Used at the END of base words (Rule 9). Its partner AI is used in the middle.
terms.exampleUsage: PLAY, DAY, and STAY have AY at the end. For the middle, use AI: RAIN, WAIT.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 2 sounds: /ay/ as in VEIN and /ee/ as in CEILING.
terms.exampleUsage: VEIN and REIN have /ay/. CEILING and RECEIVE have /ee/ (I before E except after C).

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 2 sounds: /ay/ as in THEY and /ee/ as in KEY.
terms.exampleUsage: THEY and GREY have /ay/. KEY and MONEY have /ee/.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 2 sounds: /ee/ as in FIELD and /eye/ as in PIE.
terms.exampleUsage: FIELD and THIEF have /ee/. PIE and TIE have /eye/ (usually at the end).

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 2 sounds: /oo/ as in BLUE and /yoo/ as in CUE. The E guards U since English words don't end in U alone (Rule 3).
terms.exampleUsage: BLUE and TRUE have /oo/. CUE and RESCUE have /yoo/.

terms.definition: A vowel team phonogram with 2 sounds: /oo/ as in FRUIT and /i/ as in BUILD.
terms.exampleUsage: FRUIT and JUICE have /oo/. BUILD and GUITAR have short /i/.

terms.definition: An R-controlled vowel phonogram with 1 sound: /ar/ as in CAR. The R takes control and changes A's sound completely.
terms.exampleUsage: CAR, STAR, and FARM all have AR where R controls the A sound.

terms.definition: An R-controlled vowel phonogram with 1 sound: /er/ as in HER. One of three phonograms (ER, IR, UR) that all make the same /er/ sound.
terms.exampleUsage: HER, FERN, and RIVER all have the ER sound. ER, IR, and UR all sound identical.

terms.definition: An R-controlled vowel phonogram with 1 sound: /er/ as in BIRD. Sounds exactly like ER and UR — the choice is a spelling decision, not a sound decision.
terms.exampleUsage: BIRD, GIRL, and STIR all have IR. It sounds the same as ER (HER) and UR (BURN).

terms.definition: An R-controlled vowel phonogram with 1 sound: /er/ as in BURN. The third member of the /er/ triplet with ER and IR.
terms.exampleUsage: BURN, TURN, and NURSE all have UR. Same sound as ER and IR.

terms.definition: An R-controlled vowel phonogram with 1 sound: /or/ as in FORK. R controls the O to produce a unique sound.
terms.exampleUsage: FORK, HORN, and STORM all have OR where R changes O's sound.

terms.definition: A GH combo phonogram with 1 sound: /eye/ as in LIGHT. The GH is silent — three letters represent one sound. Part of the -IGHT pattern (Rule 37).
terms.exampleUsage: LIGHT, NIGHT, and BRIGHT all have IGH. The GH is always silent here.

terms.definition: A GH combo phonogram with 1 sound: /ay/ as in EIGHT. Four letters representing one sound — the GH is silent.
terms.exampleUsage: EIGHT, WEIGH, and SLEIGH all have EIGH saying /ay/.

terms.definition: A GH combo phonogram with 6 different sounds — the trickiest phonogram in English: /oh/ (THOUGH), /oo/ (THROUGH), /ow/ (PLOUGH), /uf/ (ROUGH), /off/ (COUGH), /aw/ (THOUGHT).
terms.exampleUsage: THOUGH (/oh/), THROUGH (/oo/), ROUGH (/uf/), COUGH (/off/), THOUGHT (/aw/), PLOUGH (/ow/).

terms.definition: A GH combo phonogram with 1 sound: /aw/ as in CAUGHT. Four letters for one sound — GH is silent.
terms.exampleUsage: CAUGHT, DAUGHTER, and NAUGHTY all have AUGH saying /aw/.

terms.definition: A special phonogram with 1 sound: /sh/ as in NATION. Used at the start of suffixes in Latin-origin words. Never says /sh/ at the start of a word.
terms.exampleUsage: NATION, ACTION, and PATIENT all have TI saying /sh/ in the -TION suffix.

terms.definition: A special phonogram with 1 sound: /sh/ as in SPECIAL. Used at the start of suffixes in Latin-origin words.
terms.exampleUsage: SPECIAL, SOCIAL, and ANCIENT all have CI saying /sh/ at a suffix boundary.

terms.definition: A special phonogram with 2 sounds: /sh/ as in SESSION (unvoiced) and /zh/ as in VISION (voiced). The voiced version is unique to SI.
terms.exampleUsage: SESSION and TENSION have unvoiced /sh/. VISION and DIVISION have voiced /zh/.

terms.definition: A special phonogram with 1 sound: /sh/ as in SCIENCE. Quite rare — found in only a few words of Latin origin.
terms.exampleUsage: SCIENCE, SCISSORS, and CONSCIOUS are among the few words using SCI for /sh/.

terms.definition: A special phonogram with 3 sounds for past tense: /ed/ after T or D (WANTED), /d/ after voiced sounds (CALLED), /t/ after unvoiced sounds (JUMPED). Rule 22.
terms.exampleUsage: WANTED (/ed/ — adds syllable), CALLED (/d/ — no extra syllable), JUMPED (/t/ — no extra syllable).

terms.definition: A special phonogram with 1 sound: /kw/ as in QUEEN. Q and U are inseparable — Q never goes anywhere without U in English (Rule 11).
terms.exampleUsage: QUEEN, QUICK, and QUEST all have QU saying /kw/. No standard English word has Q without U.
